original_abstract:
This paper examines the methodological frameworks and assumptions used by European Union member states to evaluate the economic and social costs of road accidents. While EU directives advocate for the valuation of road incidents as a cornerstone of traffic safety management, the practical application of these standards varies significantly. Disparities in data accessibility across member states influence the granularity of valuation and the specific categorization of unit costs. Consequently, road accident cost estimates remain among the most difficult parameters to harmonize, complicating cross-border comparisons. 

Crucially, the most significant dimension of accident evaluation is the humanistic one—the quantification of non-material impacts such as pain, suffering, and social exclusion. This study explores the fundamental challenges associated with calculating key cost categories, prioritized by data availability, and analyzes several critical valuation metrics within a broader social context, specifically focusing on the complex determination of the value of statistical life.
humanized_abstract:
Costing methods and underlying assumptions in EU MS Other than the EU Directive requiring the economic and social valuation of road accidents for road safety management purposes, especially to assess the efficiency of measures, the EU MS have not followed the same approach. Differences in statistical availability in the Member States are at the origin of differences in terms of precision and criteria for the valuing of costs. However, the costing of road accidents remains one of the most difficult parameters to obtain an harmonised basis for comparison.

Of the many dimensions of the accident consequences, only one dimension – the humanistic dimension – is of primary importance, as it directly and/or indirectly evaluates the damage due to injuries, which imply non-material losses, such as pain, injury and loss of dignity, as well as social losses. The study deals with the basic problems which must be solved in order to evaluate the key cost items of the accident consequences according to the available information and places the values of the important valuation coefficients in the overall social context, while dealing with the methodological problems of valuation of the statistical value of human life.

This paper examines the methodological frameworks and assumptions used by European Union member states to evaluate the economic and social costs of road accidents.
